sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - The green 2x2 dot (id=34) is the player/agent that moves with arrow keys - It started inside a gray box at bottom-left and moved RIGHT by 2 columns - The gray box seems to be a starting container - Right side has two cross-shaped targets (red top, blue bottom) - Divider at col 32-33 with white dots Goal hypothesis: Navigate the green dot from left side to one of the right-side targets
- Plan: Try clicking on gray box at [30,8]-[33,11] to see if it activates something
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 31 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after clicking on the gray box at [30,8]-[33,11]. Let me check what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking gray box
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation
after_seg = after.segmentation

before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before_seg['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after_seg['nodes']}

# Check for new/removed nodes
print("New IDs:", set(after_nodes.keys()) - set(before_nodes.keys()))
print("Removed IDs:", set(before_nodes.keys()) - set(after_nodes.keys()))

# Check boundary changes
for nid in before_nodes:
    if nid in after_nodes:
        b_before = before_nodes[nid]['boundary']
        b_after = after_nodes[nid]['boundary']
        if b_before != b_after:
            print(f"Node {nid} ({before_nodes[nid]['color']}): boundary changed")
            print(f"  Before: {b_before}")
            print(f"  After: {b_after}")

# Check children changes
for nid in before_nodes:
    if nid in after_nodes:
        c_before = before_nodes[nid]['children']
        c_after = after_nodes[nid]['children']
        if c_before != c_after:
            print(f"Node {nid} ({before_nodes[nid]['color']}): children {c_before} -> {c_after}")

# Check hash changes (color/shape changes)
for nid in before_nodes:
    if nid in after_nodes:
        h_before = before_nodes[nid]['hash']
        h_after = after_nodes[nid]['hash']
        if h_before != h_after:
            print(f"Node {nid} ({before_nodes[nid]['color']}): hash changed")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Again, only the bottom bar (node 36, dark gray, and node 37, white) changed. These are HUD elements, not gameplay objects. So clicking on the gray box didn't affect the gameplay state.
Let me think about this more carefully. I've been trying many things without success. Let me reconsider the game from scratch.
The game has:
- Left side: various colored objects
- Right side: two targets (red and blue)
- Divider with white dots
- Green dot that moves with arrow keys
The interactions I've found:
1. Arrow keys move the green dot within the gray box
2. Clicking on targets (with white cross frames) collects colors
3. Clicking on fills returns colors
4. Clicking on other objects (yellow dot, dark red box, gray box) only changes HUD
Maybe the goal is to move the green dot to a specific position and then click on a target. Let me try moving the green dot to the center position and then clicking on the red target.
But wait, the white cross frames are gone, so clicking on the targets doesn't collect colors anymore. Let me check if clicking on the targets does something else now.
Actually, let me try clicking on the red fill again to see if it does something different. Maybe clicking on the fill when the colors are already in their original positions does something else.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on red fill again to see if it does something different
# Red fill is at [17,42]-[21,54], center around row 19, col 48
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 48}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
